sections.title: English has more than one future (core logic)
sections.content:
English has **no single future tense**. Instead, it picks a form based on **how the speaker sees the situation**: was it decided before, or just now? Is there evidence in front of us? Is the time fixed by a timetable, or arranged with another person?

**Why it matters (roots-first)**: This is the Verb Phrase root (#2 in the Tree Model) at work. The future is a **system of choices**, not a single tense. Czech encodes the future mainly with one form (perfective present or *budu* + infinitive), so Czech speakers often default to **will** for everything and miss the distinctions English speakers actually hear.

**The four main forms** used for future situations:
- **will** — decisions made now, predictions without evidence, offers, promises
- **going to** — plans already decided, predictions with visible evidence
- **present continuous** — fixed personal arrangements (time + place + people)
- **present simple** — public timetables and schedules

The rest of this topic walks through each one and the contrasts between them.

sections.title: will — decisions now, predictions, offers, promises
sections.content:
Use **will** when the future feeling comes *at the moment of speaking* or is a general prediction with no evidence in front of you.

**Main uses**:
- **Decision at the moment**: The phone's ringing. — *I'll get it.*
- **General prediction** (a guess, no present evidence): *I think it'll rain tomorrow. She'll probably be late.*
- **Offer**: That bag looks heavy — *I'll carry it for you.*
- **Promise / refusal**: *I'll be there at 5. I won't tell anyone.*

**Often signalled by**: *I think, I hope, probably, maybe, I'm sure, I bet*.

**Contrast — same situation, decided when?**
- *I'll have the soup, please.* (deciding now, looking at the menu)
- *I'm going to have the soup.* (decided earlier — odd if the waiter just arrived)

**Czech-speaker note**: Don't default to **will** for plans you already made. *I will meet John tomorrow* sounds like you decided this second. If it's arranged, use **going to** or the present continuous.

sections.title: going to — plans and visible evidence
sections.content:
Use **going to** when the intention exists *before* you speak, or when the prediction is based on something you can see right now.

**Main uses**:
- **Plan / intention already decided**: *I'm going to study medicine. We're going to buy a new car next year.*
- **Prediction from present evidence**: *Look at those clouds — it's going to rain. He's running for the bus but it's leaving — he's going to miss it.*

**Contrast pair — will vs going to**:
- *I'll rain tomorrow* — wrong; with a guess about tomorrow, no evidence → *It'll rain tomorrow.*
- *Look at the sky — it will rain* — wrong; evidence is visible → *It's going to rain.*
- *The phone's ringing — I'm going to get it* — wrong; deciding now → *I'll get it.*

**Rule of thumb**: *going to* points backwards (plan decided before) or to something visible (evidence now). *will* points to the moment of speaking.

sections.title: present continuous — fixed arrangements
sections.content:
Use the **present continuous** (*am/is/are + -ing*) for future **arrangements** — plans already locked in with another person, a specific time, or a specific place.

**The key word is arrangement**: someone else is involved or the slot is booked.

**Examples**:
- *I'm meeting Tom at 6 tomorrow.* (we agreed; the time is set)
- *She's flying to London on Friday.* (ticket bought)
- *We're having dinner with my parents on Sunday.* (planned with them)

**Contrast — going to vs present continuous**:
- *I'm going to learn Italian.* (intention; no specific arrangement yet)
- *I'm starting an Italian course on Monday.* (signed up — fixed arrangement)

Both are often interchangeable for personal plans; the present continuous is more natural when the arrangement is concrete.

**Czech-speaker note**: Czech speakers often reach for **will** here (*I will meet Tom tomorrow*). Native speakers would normally say *I'm meeting Tom tomorrow* — the arrangement already exists, so it feels almost present, not future.

sections.title: present simple — timetables and schedules
sections.content:
Use the **present simple** for future events run by a **timetable, schedule, or programme** — things outside your personal control.

**Examples**:
- *The train leaves at 6 a.m. tomorrow.*
- *The film starts at 8.*
- *The shops close at 7 on Sunday.*
- *Our flight arrives at 22:40.*

**Key test**: Could you read this off a board, screen, or printed schedule? Then use the present simple.

**Contrast — present simple vs present continuous**:
- *The train leaves at 6.* (public timetable)
- *I'm leaving at 6.* (your personal arrangement)

**Czech-speaker note**: Czech speakers sometimes extend the present simple to personal plans — *I go to Paris tomorrow* sounds wrong in English. For personal plans use **going to** or the **present continuous**: *I'm going to Paris tomorrow.* Save the present simple for public timetables.

sections.title: The four futures side by side
sections.content:
One scenario — *meeting a friend tomorrow at 6* — across all four forms. Same situation, four different speaker meanings.

**Why it matters**: The point of this topic is the **choice**, not the forms in isolation. Once you can feel why a native speaker picks one over the others, the rest is practice.

**The same scenario, four readings**:
- *I'll meet you at 6.* — deciding now (or making a promise / offer)
- *I'm going to meet a friend at 6.* — the plan existed before; no specific arrangement mentioned
- *I'm meeting a friend at 6.* — fixed arrangement (we agreed)
- *I meet my friend at 6.* — only natural for a recurring schedule (e.g. *Every Tuesday I meet my friend at 6*); for a one-off, English uses the present continuous or *going to*

The table below summarises the typical use of each form.

diagram.title: The four futures — when to use each
rows: Decision now, prediction (no evidence), offer, promise
I'll get it. I think it'll rain. I'll help you.
Plan already decided; prediction from visible evidence
I'm going to study medicine. Look — it's going to rain.

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors
sections.content:
Three recurring mistakes — fixing them gives immediate, audible progress.

**1. Defaulting to *will* for arranged plans**
- Wrong: *I will meet John tomorrow at 5.* (already arranged)
- Right: *I'm meeting John tomorrow at 5.* / *I'm going to meet John tomorrow at 5.*

**2. Present simple for personal plans**
- Wrong: *I go to Paris tomorrow.*
- Right: *I'm going to Paris tomorrow.* / *I'm going to go to Paris tomorrow.*
- The present simple is reserved for **public timetables** (*The train leaves at 6*), not your own plans.

**3. *will* with visible evidence**
- Wrong: *Look at the sky — it will rain.*
- Right: *Look at the sky — it's going to rain.*
- If the cause is in front of you, use **going to**.

**Why it matters**: These three errors mark a speaker as B1-stuck. Fixing them is a clean step up — the grammar is simple, the choice is the work.

**Next steps**: practise the contrasts in the Check and Practice sections below. The future continuous and future perfect (*I'll be working / I'll have finished*) are separate topics — covered under Tenses: General.

questions.question: You already decided yesterday to see a film tonight. Which do you say?

options.a: I'm going to see a film tonight.
options.b: I will see a film tonight.

questions.question: The phone rings. You decide at that moment to answer it. Which do you say?
questions.explanation: Decision at the moment of speaking → will.

questions.question: You can see black clouds. You predict rain. Which is natural?
questions.explanation: Prediction based on present evidence (you can see the clouds) → going to.

questions.question: You want to offer to carry someone's bag. Which do you say?

options.b: I'm going to carry your bag.

questions.question: You have a general opinion about tomorrow's weather (no clouds now). Which is natural?

options.a: I think it's going to rain tomorrow.
options.c: I think it will rain tomorrow.
options.d: I think it will be raining tomorrow.

questions.explanation: General prediction (no present evidence), often with I think → will.

questions.question: I ____ (see) the dentist tomorrow at 10. (I already have an appointment.)
The phone's ringing. – I ____ (get) it.
questions.explanation: Decision at the moment of speaking → will.

questions.question: Look at those clouds – it ____ (rain).
questions.explanation: Prediction from present evidence → going to.

questions.question: I'm so tired. I ____ (have) a rest. (I just decided.)
She ____ (study) law at university. (She decided last year.)
That bag looks heavy. I ____ (carry) it for you.
questions.explanation: Offer: both will (I'll do it) and can (I'm able to) are natural here.

questions.question: We ____ (meet) at 6. (We arranged it yesterday.)
I think the match ____ (be) exciting.
He's running towards the bus but it's leaving – he ____ (miss) it.
questions.explanation: Prediction from what we can see now → going to.

questions.question: I promise I ____ (not tell) anyone.
They ____ (buy) a new car next year. (They've been saving for it.)
I don't think she ____ (come) to the party.

errorcorrection.title: Practice: Correct the form (will vs going to)

questions.question: Wrong: 'I will meet John at 5.' (You already arranged it.) Correct the sentence.
answers: I'm going to meet John at 5.
I am going to meet John at 5.
I'm going to meet John at 5
I am going to meet John at 5

questions.question: Wrong: (Phone rings) 'I'm going to get it.' (You decide at that moment.) Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'Look at the sky – it will rain.' (You can see the clouds.) Correct the sentence.
answers: Look at the sky – it's going to rain.
Look at the sky – it is going to rain.
Look at the sky – it's going to rain
Look at the sky – it is going to rain
questions.explanation: Prediction from present evidence → going to. Contractions (it's) and full form (it is) are both accepted.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I'm going to help you.' (You are offering now.) Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'I think it's going to be sunny tomorrow.' (No special evidence, just your guess.) Correct the sentence.
answers: I think it will be sunny tomorrow.
I think it'll be sunny tomorrow.
I think it will be sunny tomorrow
I think it'll be sunny tomorrow
questions.explanation: General prediction with I think → will. (Going to is also possible but will is more common for a simple guess.)

questions.question: Wrong: 'She will study in Prague.' (She decided and applied last month.) Correct the sentence.
answers: She's going to study in Prague.
She is going to study in Prague.
She's going to study in Prague
She is going to study in Prague

mc.title: Practice: Will and going to (multiple choice)
